**Why has this happened?**  
Nvidia, a business built upon the production of consumer graphics cards for gaming and professional workloads has increasingly moved away from this business.  
As of Nvidia's Q1 FY2027 results, the company will no longer report sales of its consumer graphics cards. (Tom’s Hardware, 2026a) While the change is just a reporting decision, this sends a clear message that its primary business is artificial intelligence and data-centre hardware, not graphics.  
Rising GPU costs from an AI-driven pricing crisis have made gaming PCs more inaccessible. This has caused firms producing motherboards (a component required for a PC’s architecture), like Asus, to experience an unprecedented fall in sales. Asus, which sold 15 million units in 2025, will struggle to move 10 million by the end of 2026 as it shipped little more than 5 million in the first half of 2026. (Tom’s Hardware, 2026b)  
Gigabyte, another motherboard producer, has dramatically pivoted production towards AI servers, as opposed to serving consumers, with servers reaching around 70% of its revenue in early 2026. (BigGo Finance, 2026)  
This sends a signal to hardware component producers that they must move away from serving consumers and rather to capitalise on hyperscaler data-centre spending. Firms unable to adapt are likely to go out of business.  
Over time, these factors would cause the consumer motherboard market and other computer hardware markets alike to become increasingly concentrated with firms more able to charge higher prices which would contract the market for low and mid-range PCs.  
The rise in prices of gaming PCs has been exacerbated by a rise in memory prices with combined DRAM and SSD prices forecast to surge 130% by the end of 2026. This will cause memory costs to climb from 16% to 23% of a PC’s total bill of materials this year. (Tom’s Hardware, 2026c)

The lower-range gaming PC market is likely to all but disappear. As the gaming computer component market becomes increasingly concentrated and smaller, firms will no longer enjoy the same economies of scale of a market with a booming amount of demand. This will push the entry price of a gaming computer beyond the reach of any consumer without significant disposable income.  
While Jensen Huang, the CEO of Nvidia, insisted the gaming PC was "here to stay" (Trusted Reviews, 2019), his vision now points in another direction. At CES 2026, he suggested that large jumps in raw rendering power may be over. (Club386, 2026) This means a system's performance would increasingly depend on Nvidia's AI software rather than the hardware the consumer controls. This, along with rising costs, would push consumers toward cloud platforms such as GeForce NOW.

**Is this good?**  
A rise of cloud computing through services like Nvidia GeForce NOW allows consumers who cannot afford a whole gaming rig to play AAA games for a fraction of the upfront cost, giving them a way to play virtually any modern game on their existing hardware.  
This could expand the market rather than shrink it. A player would only need a screen and connection. This majorly lowers the cost of entry and brings in people previously entirely priced out. This would be a frictionless trade for consumers used to paying subscriptions for streaming film, music and television. Gaming would become no different.

**Is this bad?**  
This shift also spells the end of genuine ownership within the industry. Under a subscription, the player no longer owns the machine; rather they would rent access to hardware sitting in a data-centre on the terms of the corporation that runs it. Prices can rise, libraries can shrink and access can be revoked, especially if the cloud computing market is dominated by only a small number of firms.  
The experience would also be dependent on the quality of an internet connection which reintroduces an inequality of access. Crucially, the hobbyist culture of building and upgrading a PC, a huge part of why the enthusiast market exists, would cease to exist as the tech starts sitting in a building you will never enter.

**Who will it affect?**  
The clearest losers are budget and mid-range PC gamers. For many, a $700 \- $1000 build was once viable as an entry point to the hobby. As that price tier disappears, the community shrinks too.  
The other losers are the companies who serve them. Firms like Gigabyte, with the resources to pivot towards AI, can follow the money out of the consumer market. But for smaller firms, there may be no data-centre businesses to retreat into. They are squeezed from both sides: rising component costs they cannot absorb and a shrinking consumer base that can no longer afford their products. Ultimately, this leaves a market with fewer competitors, leading to a small group of price-setting firms, causing the market to become even more concentrated.
